Were You Asked This?
Design a Webhook Callback System

Design a webhook system that allows applications to register callback URLs and automatically receive HTTP POST notifications when specific events occur in other applications.

Asked at:

Snowflake

OpenAI

Stripe webhooks are HTTP callbacks that notify your application when events happen (e.g., a payment succeeds), and GitHub webhooks do the same for repository events. A webhook callback system generalizes this idea: third‑party apps register URLs and event subscriptions, and your platform reliably POSTs event payloads to those URLs as events occur.

Interviewers ask this to test how you design reliable, secure, multi‑tenant event fan‑out under failure. You’ll need to reason about at‑least‑once delivery, retries and dead‑letter queues, tenant isolation and backpressure, idempotency, ordering guarantees, and payload signing. The problem looks simple on the surface ("send an HTTP POST"), but strong candidates uncover edge cases and propose pragmatic controls that keep the system safe at scale.

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to register and manage callback endpoints and select which event types they want to receive.

Users should be able to receive signed HTTPS POST notifications for subscribed events with basic delivery guarantees (at‑least‑once).

Users should be able to see delivery history, including success/failure status, response codes, and payloads (with safe redaction).

Users should be able to test, pause/resume, and retry deliveries for a specific endpoint or subscription.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How will you ensure reliable, scalable delivery (retries, ordering, and fan‑out) as event volume reaches millions per hour?

How do you secure webhook deliveries and prevent spoofing, tampering, and replay attacks?

How will you handle tenant isolation and backpressure so one misbehaving or slow endpoint doesn’t degrade the entire system?

How do you evolve event schemas without breaking consumers and support replays for missed events?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Real-time Updates

Webhooks are an out-of-band real-time notification mechanism. The system must capture events, fan them out quickly to external endpoints, and provide low-latency delivery without coupling producers to consumers.

Read More
Scaling Writes

Each event can fan out to thousands of endpoints, creating a write-heavy workload of outbound HTTP requests and delivery records. You need partitioned queues, worker pools, and backpressure to scale the write path.

Read More
Multi-step Processes

Delivery involves a workflow: enqueue, attempt, retry with backoff, mark success/failure, and potentially dead-letter or pause endpoints. Modeling this as a durable multi-step process improves resilience and observability.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka provides durable, partitioned logs for decoupling event producers from delivery workers, preserving per-endpoint ordering (via keying), buffering bursts, and enabling dead-letter topics for failures.

Read More
Redis

Redis excels at fast, ephemeral state: per-tenant rate limiting tokens, dedup caches for idempotency, distributed locks, and short-lived delivery state that keeps hot paths efficient.

Read More
PostgreSQL

PostgreSQL is a solid fit for managing webhook endpoints, secrets, subscriptions, delivery metadata, and audit trails with strong consistency and transactional updates for admin operations.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Payment System

Payment platforms like Stripe rely heavily on webhooks for event notifications (charges, disputes). They share needs for secure payload signing, retries, idempotency, and auditability.

Read
Practice
Rate Limiter

A webhook platform must apply per-tenant and per-endpoint rate limits and backpressure to prevent noisy neighbors from overwhelming shared infrastructure.

Read
Practice
Job Scheduler

Reliable delivery with exponential backoff, dead-lettering, and pause/resume semantics mirrors scheduling and retry patterns found in distributed job orchestration systems.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Synchronous fan-out in the producer request path

Blocking the event producer while POSTing to many endpoints couples availability and latency to external systems. A single slow or failing consumer can take down your producer SLOs.

No payload signing, replay protection, or TLS enforcement

Without HMAC signatures, timestamps, and HTTPS, attackers can spoof or tamper with events. This leads to fraud, data leaks, and broken integrations.

Promising exactly-once delivery without idempotency and dead-letter handling

Networks make duplicates inevitable. If you don’t design for at-least-once with idempotency keys, retries, and DLQs, you will lose events or cause double-processing in receivers.
